patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 27-year-old anxious but clear-speaking male with hemophilia. Start with: your right knee became suddenly swollen, warm, very painful since yesterday, hard to bend or bear weight, and you don’t recall injury. Volunteer early: you have hemophilia, you’re worried it’s a joint bleed, you’ve had on-and-off nosebleeds this week that take long to stop, and you bruise easily. If asked, add: rapid worsening over hours, tight “filling up” feeling, prior similar ankle/elbow swelling, gum bleeding with brushing, heavy bleeding after dental cleaning 10 days ago, missed a couple prophylactic factor doses due to delayed refill, and you took ibuprofen last night. Mention once: dark cola-colored urine occurred once last month. Withhold unless directly asked with privacy reassurance: bleeding after sex, intentionally skipping factor to “save it,” and weekend heavy drinking. If asked about other issues, mention chronic knee ache/stiffness for months, avoiding stairs/sports, occasional foot tingling when swollen, fatigue, and headaches after minor head bumps. Express misconceptions: worry it’s infection (warmth/chills), possible gout (meat/seafood), and think stretching factor/underreporting protects finances/insurance; consider cheaper product as equivalent. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears, and become more forthcoming.

medications: Efmoroctocog alfa (recombinant factor VIII Fc) 50 IU/kg IV twice weekly for prophylaxis
Tranexamic acid 1 g PO every 8 hours as needed for mucosal bleeding (avoid if gross hematuria)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 3 g/day)
confounders: Low-titer factor VIII inhibitor causing incomplete response to replacement therapy
Recent use of NSAIDs for joint pain increasing bleeding tendency
Chronic hemophilic arthropathy with baseline joint swelling/pain that can mimic acute hemarthrosis
Possible von Willebrand factor deficiency overlap considered when factor VIII levels fluctuate (requires vWF testing if atypical)

acceptable_alternatives: Hemophilia A with acute bleeding episode
Severe hemophilia A (if history suggests factor VIII activity <1%)
required_evidence: Personal history of excessive/prolonged bleeding (e.g., hemarthroses, muscle hematomas, prolonged bleeding after procedures) with normal platelet count
Isolated prolonged aPTT with normal PT/INR and normal thrombin time
Clinical context of known hemophilia treated with recombinant factor VIII (efmoroctocog alfa) or clear prior diagnosis documentation
No evidence for disseminated intravascular coagulation (normal fibrinogen and platelets) to explain bleeding

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be decreased if significant/ongoing bleeding; can be normal if no major blood loss
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: May be decreased if significant/ongoing bleeding; can be normal if no major blood loss
expected_lab_results.WBC count: Typically normal (may be elevated with stress/inflammation if acute bleed)

medications: Efmoroctocog alfa (recombinant factor VIII, extended half-life) 50 IU/kg IV once for suspected serious bleed, then 25-50 IU/kg IV every 12-24 hours based on clinical response and bleeding severity
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain (max 3000 mg/day; adjust for liver disease/alcohol use)
non_pharmacological: Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs and other antiplatelet agents due to increased bleeding risk
RICE for joint/muscle bleed (rest, ice, compression, elevation) and immobilize affected joint if hemarthrosis suspected
Use local measures for minor bleeding (pressure, topical hemostatic measures where appropriate)
If head/neck/abdominal pain, neurologic symptoms, or major trauma: treat immediately with factor and arrange urgent ED evaluation/imaging
Coordinate care with hemophilia treatment center/hematology; ensure access plan for home infusion if applicable
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology/hemophilia treatment center follow-up within 24-72 hours after acute bleed treatment (same-day if severe/at-risk bleed); reassess symptoms and consider factor level monitoring per specialist protocol

must_include: Hemophilia A is due to low/absent factor VIII causing impaired clot formation
Labs typically show isolated prolonged aPTT with normal PT/INR and platelets
Bleeding can occur into joints/muscles and can be serious even without obvious external bleeding
Factor VIII replacement (efmoroctocog alfa) treats bleeding by restoring clotting factor
Treat suspected serious bleeds promptly—do not wait for imaging/labs if symptoms suggest high-risk bleeding
How IV dosing frequency may change based on bleed severity and response, and when to seek urgent care
Review warning signs requiring emergency evaluation (head injury, severe headache, neurologic changes, neck/throat swelling, severe abdominal pain, uncontrolled bleeding)
Discuss potential complications (recurrent joint damage, inhibitor development) and importance of specialist follow-up
Confirm understanding of aspirin allergy and reinforce avoidance of aspirin-containing products
Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs; use acetaminophen for pain unless contraindicated
Bleed prevention strategies (protective gear, safe physical activities, prompt treatment of bleeds)
Carry medical alert identification and share hemophilia diagnosis and treatment plan with other clinicians/dentists

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit bleeding history and acuity (site, severity, trauma/head injury), hemophilia type/severity, inhibitor history, home factor use, last dose, weight, and current meds/allergies to determine urgency and appropriate factor dosing.

Order baseline/confirmatory labs when appropriate (aPTT, PT/INR, CBC/platelets; factor VIII activity and inhibitor/Bethesda if not known) while emphasizing that treatment of suspected serious bleeding should not be delayed.
Review results to support diagnosis (isolated prolonged aPTT with normal PT/platelets) and to identify anemia or other abnormalities; factor levels/inhibitor results guide longer-term management.
Diagnose hemophilia A-related bleeding (or suspected bleed in known hemophilia A) and stratify bleed risk (e.g., intracranial, neck/throat, GI, iliopsoas, large muscle/joint).
Perform required critical safety check before prescribing; confirm aspirin allergy and any prior reactions to factor products/excipients.
Assess for interacting/contraindicated agents (e.g., antiplatelets, anticoagulants, NSAIDs) and document risk mitigation; non-critical but expected.
Prescribe factor VIII replacement (efmoroctocog alfa) with dose/frequency adjusted to bleed severity and response; ensure urgent treatment for high-risk bleeds without waiting for imaging/labs.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment plan and urgency, address concerns/warning signs, and provide lifestyle/medication avoidance counseling including aspirin/NSAIDs.
Arrange hematology/hemophilia treatment center follow-up; discuss monitoring for inhibitors, joint health, and prophylaxis planning.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 62-year-old male long-haul truck driver. You speak vaguely and are a bit evasive, focusing on “really bad headaches,” intermittent blurry vision (worse with headaches), sweating more than before, and that your hands/feet seem bigger (rings/shoes don’t fit). Mention your face “looks different” but don’t elaborate unless pressed.

If asked directly, admit jaw feels bigger, voice deeper/thicker, tingling/numb hands at night, joint aches, loud snoring with occasional gasping, daytime fatigue, weight gain, lower sex drive. Avoid bringing up erectile problems, avoiding photos, mood/irritability unless the clinician builds strong rapport.

Do not volunteer that hat/glove sizes increased, thirst/urination increased, slow healing, oily skin/skin tags, or teeth spreading unless specifically asked.

Express misconceptions: you suspect allergies (puffy lips/eyelids), vitamin/nerve issue for tingling, or “swelling inside” for belly cramps.

Push for a quick/cheap fix; worry about bills, insurance denial, and losing commercial driving eligibility. If met with empathy, soften slightly but still minimize and ask to “wait and see.”

medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily
lisinopril 20 mg once daily
atorvastatin 40 mg once daily
aspirin 81 mg once daily
omeprazole 20 mg once daily
confounders: poorly controlled obstructive sleep apnea contributing to fatigue, headaches, and hypertension
type 2 diabetes and obesity causing insulin resistance and elevated IGF-1 interpretation complexity
chronic proton pump inhibitor use potentially masking upper GI symptoms (e.g., reflux) and delaying evaluation
possible ectopic source of GHRH (e.g., bronchial or pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) mimicking pituitary acromegaly and requiring targeted imaging

correct_diagnosis.primary: GRFoma (ectopic GHRH-secreting neuroendocrine tumor causing acromegaly)
acceptable_alternatives: Acromegaly due to pituitary somatotroph adenoma (if GHRH not elevated and pituitary lesion present)
required_evidence: Elevated age-adjusted serum IGF-1
Failure of GH suppression on 75 g OGTT (GH nadir not suppressed to normal)
Elevated plasma GHRH (supports ectopic source)
Clinical syndrome consistent with acromegaly (progressive acral/facial changes and/or related complications)
Exclude primary hypothyroidism as a cause of elevated IGF-1/GH axis abnormalities (TSH/Free T4 not consistent with untreated hypothyroidism)
Assess for co-secreted pituitary hormones (e.g., prolactin) and adrenal/thyroid status prior to definitive therapy

expected_lab_results.Serum IGF-1: Elevated above age- and sex-adjusted reference range
expected_lab_results.Random growth hormone (GH): Elevated and/or not appropriately low (random value may be high/variable)
expected_lab_results.Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) 75 g: Fails to suppress; GH nadir remains above assay-specific normal suppression threshold (commonly >1 ng/mL; in many modern ultrasensitive assays >0.4 ng/mL)
expected_lab_results.Plasma GHRH: Elevated (typically clearly above the upper limit of normal; supports ectopic GHRH secretion)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Normal to mildly elevated (can be elevated due to pituitary hyperplasia/stalk effect; not required to be high)
expected_lab_results.Morning cortisol (8 AM): Normal (no biochemical adrenal insufficiency expected unless other pathology present)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Normal (or not consistent with untreated primary hypothyroidism)

medications: Octreotide LAR 20 mg IM every 4 weeks (titrate 20-30 mg based on IGF-1/GH response and tolerability) OR Lanreotide depot 90 mg deep SQ every 4 weeks (titrate 60-120 mg)
If inadequate biochemical control after optimized somatostatin analog: Pegvisomant 10 mg SQ daily (titrate by 5 mg increments to normalize IGF-1; monitor LFTs)

If tumor is metastatic/progressive neuroendocrine tumor and somatostatin-receptor positive: consider additional oncologic therapy per NET guidelines (e.g., PRRT, targeted therapy) in coordination with oncology (not a single default regimen)
non_pharmacological: Localize and resect the ectopic GHRH-secreting tumor when feasible (definitive therapy; surgical referral after imaging localization such as CT/MRI chest/abdomen/pelvis ± functional imaging based on NET workup)
If pituitary enlargement is present, treat the ectopic source first; avoid unnecessary pituitary surgery unless a true pituitary adenoma is confirmed

Screen and manage acromegaly complications: blood pressure, diabetes (A1c/fasting glucose), sleep apnea evaluation, cardiomyopathy assessment (ECG/echo if indicated), colonoscopy per acromegaly recommendations, arthropathy management
Lifestyle counseling for cardiometabolic risk reduction (diet, exercise, weight management, smoking cessation if applicable)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess symptoms and repeat IGF-1 (and GH as needed) 8-12 weeks after starting or changing medical therapy; then every 3-6 months until controlled, and every 6-12 months once stable. Post-resection: check IGF-1 and GH/OGTT at ~12 weeks.

must_include: Labs show excess growth hormone effect (high IGF-1 and failure of GH suppression on glucose test)
Elevated GHRH suggests the driver is an ectopic GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma) rather than a primary pituitary tumor
Need to localize the source tumor with imaging and treat it to cure the hormone excess
Discuss key complications of uncontrolled acromegaly and why timely treatment matters
Definitive plan is surgical removal of the ectopic tumor when possible
Medical therapy (somatostatin analogs ± pegvisomant) can control hormones/symptoms before surgery or if not resectable
Monitoring plan: repeat IGF-1 and clinical response at defined intervals; adjust doses based on results
Common adverse effects and monitoring: gallstones/GI effects and glucose changes with somatostatin analogs; liver tests with pegvisomant
Elicit patient priorities and symptoms (appearance changes, headaches, joint pain, sweating, sleep issues)
Explain uncertainty and next steps if tumor localization is not immediately successful
Review allergy to penicillin and reassure it does not limit the main hormone-control medications
Discuss fertility/sexual function and menstrual/testosterone issues if relevant, and offer appropriate evaluation
Cardiometabolic risk reduction (diet, exercise, weight management) to help blood pressure and glucose
Sleep apnea awareness and referral if symptoms (snoring, daytime sleepiness) are present
Importance of follow-up labs and imaging to prevent long-term complications
When to seek urgent care (new severe headache/vision changes, chest pain, severe shortness of breath)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms, duration, functional impact, red flags (headache/vision changes), and patient priorities; screen for cardiometabolic and sleep apnea symptoms relevant to acromegaly/GRFoma.

Order/confirm biochemical evidence and baseline comorbidity assessment: IGF-1, GH with oral glucose suppression test if not already done, GHRH level, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, CMP/LFTs, prolactin and other pituitary axes as clinically indicated; pregnancy test if relevant.
Review results to support diagnosis (elevated IGF-1, lack of GH suppression, elevated GHRH) and to establish baseline safety for therapy (LFTs, glucose).
State diagnosis of acromegaly due to ectopic GHRH-secreting tumor (GRFoma) and frame next steps: localize tumor and treat definitively.

Explain diagnosis and complications; discuss localization plan (cross-sectional imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis, somatostatin receptor imaging such as Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT; pituitary MRI may show hyperplasia). Address uncertainty if initial localization is negative and outline iterative approach.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm allergies (including penicillin allergy details) and verify no allergy to planned agents (somatostatin analogs, pegvisomant).
Optional safety check before prescribing: review current medications for interactions (e.g., glucose-lowering meds adjustments, QT-risk meds if relevant, cyclosporine with octreotide).

Initiate medical therapy when indicated (pre-op control, symptomatic disease, or unresectable/unknown primary): long-acting somatostatin analog (octreotide LAR or lanreotide) ± pegvisomant if IGF-1 not controlled; include monitoring for gallstones/GI effects, glucose changes, and LFT monitoring for pegvisomant.

Explain treatment plan: definitive surgical removal of ectopic tumor when localized/resectable; expected benefits/risks; monitoring schedule for IGF-1 and symptoms; adverse effects and when to seek care; reassure penicillin allergy does not limit main hormone-control meds.

Arrange follow-up for lab reassessment and dose titration (e.g., IGF-1 in 8–12 weeks after starting/adjusting somatostatin analog; LFTs for pegvisomant baseline and periodically), and coordinate imaging/surgical referral; address comorbidity referrals (sleep study, cardiology/endocrinology).
Close encounter after confirming patient understanding, safety-net instructions, and follow-up plan.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms, timeline, diabetes history, insulin use/adherence, possible triggers (infection, missed insulin), and red flags (altered mental status, chest pain, dyspnea).
Assess volume status and severity clues (vomiting, oral intake, urine output), and screen for infectious symptoms (fever, cough, dysuria) or other precipitants (MI, pancreatitis, steroids).

Order confirmatory and severity labs for suspected DKA: POC glucose, BMP (Na/K/Cl/HCO3/BUN/Cr), venous/arterial blood gas, serum beta-hydroxybutyrate (or ketones), anion gap, serum osmolality, CBC, UA/urine ketones, LFTs, lipase, lactate if ill-appearing, pregnancy test if applicable.
Order evaluation for precipitants and complications: ECG, troponin if chest pain/risk, blood cultures if febrile, chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms, urine culture if urinary symptoms.
Interpret results to confirm diagnosis and grade severity (anion gap metabolic acidosis, hyperglycemia, ketonemia/ketonuria, potassium level, renal function, osmolality).
Make diagnosis (e.g., diabetic ketoacidosis) and identify likely trigger(s); document severity and need for ICU-level monitoring.
Explain diagnosis in patient-friendly terms, including acidosis from ketones due to insufficient insulin, dangers of dehydration/electrolyte shifts, and need for confirmatory tests and frequent labs.
Explain treatment plan: IV fluids, IV insulin, potassium monitoring/replacement, and later addition of dextrose while insulin continues until acidosis resolves.
Before prescribing any medications (especially antibiotics), verify allergies (including penicillin allergy details and reaction type) to ensure safe selection.
Before prescribing, check for clinically relevant interactions with current meds (e.g., QT-prolonging agents, nephrotoxic drugs) and planned therapies.

Initiate guideline-consistent DKA management orders (IV isotonic fluids, IV insulin infusion protocol after potassium assessment/repletion, electrolyte replacement plan) and treat precipitating cause if identified; ensure contraindications are checked first.

Address concerns: penicillin allergy implications for antibiotics if infection suspected; review urgent warning signs; set expectations for ICU monitoring and typical duration/criteria for transition off insulin drip.

Provide lifestyle/sick-day education: do not stop basal insulin, increase glucose/ketone checks, hydration, when to seek care, adherence and follow-up plan, and avoiding triggers (missed insulin, dehydration, prompt infection treatment).
Arrange diabetes follow-up after stabilization (endocrinology/primary care), education reinforcement, and review of insulin regimen and supplies.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, clear plan, and follow-up.

general_concerns: I thought ataxia is basically the same as Parkinson’s, so I assumed I’ll need the same medications and it will definitely cause dementia.
If my MRI doesn’t show a big problem, then it can’t be a real neurological disease—maybe it’s just stress or anxiety.
I read that gluten-free diets and vitamin supplements can reverse cerebellar damage, so if I’m strict enough I might cure it.
Since it’s genetic, I assumed symptoms will appear suddenly and then I’ll end up in a wheelchair within a year no matter what I do.
If I start physical therapy too early, I might ‘wear out’ my nerves and make the progression faster.
economic_concerns: Genetic testing sounds expensive, and I’m worried my insurance will refuse it or raise my premiums if results show a hereditary condition.
I’m concerned that if this diagnosis is documented, I could lose my job or be denied disability coverage later.
I assume PT/OT is optional and not worth the copays because it won’t change the disease, so I might skip it to save money.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 42-year-old female accounting clerk. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve been getting more and more unsteady on my feet, like I’m going to lose my balance.” Volunteer early: frequent stumbling (worse turning/at night), occasional slurred speech without drinking, and clumsy hands (buttons/writing). If asked about timeline, say it’s slowly worsened over a couple years. If asked about falls, say you’ve fallen at home and avoid stairs. If asked about coordination/vision/swallowing, mention overshooting when reaching, “bouncy” vision while walking, and coughing with thin liquids. If asked about family history, say a parent had “balance problems” later in life. Do not volunteer fatigue/heavy legs, unsteadiness with eyes closed, trouble tracking moving text, or numb/tingly feet unless specifically asked. Act embarrassed and initially minimize urinary accidents and lowered sex drive; only admit if directly asked in a private, nonjudgmental way. Express misconceptions: wonder about inner ear issues (no spinning), worry people think you’re drinking, consider anxiety. Raise cost/insurance/job worries about genetic testing and assume PT/OT isn’t worth copays. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share more details.

Order basic reversible-cause screening appropriate to an initial ataxia evaluation (e.g., CBC, CMP, TSH, vitamin B12, vitamin E; consider A1c and celiac serology as clinically indicated).
Review lab results to ensure no obvious reversible metabolic/nutritional cause is missed before anchoring on hereditary SCA.

Provide working diagnosis (suspected spinocerebellar ataxia vs hereditary cerebellar ataxia) based on chronic progressive cerebellar syndrome and history; emphasize confirmation via neurology evaluation and potential genetic testing.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, expected course, and urgent red flags; address patient concerns including inheritance and daily-life safety.
Explain treatment plan focusing on rehabilitation (PT/OT/SLP), fall prevention, assistive devices, swallowing precautions, and lifestyle modifications (avoid alcohol/sedatives).
Arrange neurology referral, discuss/arrange genetic counseling/testing pathway, and set follow-up to review results and safety planning.
If any medication is being considered/prescribed, verify allergies (penicillin: hives; codeine: severe N/V) before prescribing (critical safety check).
If prescribing, check for drug-drug and drug-substance interactions (especially sedatives/alcohol) (non-critical but expected).
Only if clinically indicated for symptom management; avoid sedating agents when possible and avoid codeine/penicillin-class drugs given allergies; ensure contraindications are assessed first.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety plan, and follow-up arrangements.

acceptable_alternatives: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), likely idiopathic pending exclusion of secondary causes

required_evidence: Progressive exertional dyspnea and/or fatigue with signs of right heart strain (e.g., exertional presyncope/syncope, peripheral edema) without an alternative pulmonary parenchymal explanation
Elevated BNP and/or NT-proBNP consistent with right ventricular strain
Arterial blood gas on room air showing mild hypoxemia with low/normal PaCO2 (hyperventilation pattern) rather than hypercapnia
No biochemical evidence of acute coronary syndrome (high-sensitivity troponin I not showing dynamic rise/fall consistent with MI)

Clinical picture not consistent with infectious pneumonia (no expected acute febrile illness) or eosinophilic pneumonia (no peripheral eosinophilia provided; would expect hypoxemia with systemic features) or cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (would expect subacute cough/constitutional symptoms and imaging findings—requires exclusion clinically)

expected_lab_results.BNP: Elevated above normal range (suggests right ventricular strain; degree correlates with severity)
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Elevated above normal range (supports right heart strain; higher values suggest worse prognosis)
expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): Normal or mildly elevated without dynamic rise/fall; significant elevation would suggest RV ischemia/strain or alternative diagnosis such as ACS
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) pH: Normal to mildly alkalemic (respiratory alkalosis possible)
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) PaO2: Mildly reduced (hypoxemia can be present; often not profoundly low in isolated PAH)
expected_lab_results.Arterial blood gas (room air) HCO3-: Normal to mildly reduced (compensation for respiratory alkalosis if present)
expected_lab_results.Pulse oximetry (room air): Normal to mildly decreased (often low 90s; may be normal at rest early)

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily, titrate to euvolemia (if volume overloaded/right heart failure signs)
Spironolactone 12.5-25 mg PO daily (adjunct diuresis if congestion; monitor K+ and renal function)

Tadalafil 40 mg PO daily OR Sildenafil 20 mg PO three times daily (PAH-specific therapy; choose one PDE-5 inhibitor)
Ambrisentan 5 mg PO daily, increase to 10 mg daily as tolerated (endothelin receptor antagonist; use as combination therapy when appropriate)
Epoprostenol IV continuous infusion (dose titrated by PAH center) OR Treprostinil (IV/SC/inhaled) for high-risk/advanced disease or inadequate response to oral therapy
Warfarin (target INR per PAH center protocol) ONLY if another indication exists (e.g., atrial fibrillation, VTE) or center-specific use; not routine for all idiopathic PAH
non_pharmacological: Immediate referral to a pulmonary hypertension specialty center for confirmatory right heart catheterization and risk stratification before definitive PAH-targeted escalation
Assess and manage volume status; daily weights and sodium restriction (e.g., 2 g/day) if congested
Supervised exercise/rehabilitation once stable; avoid heavy isometric exertion and activities provoking syncope

Avoid high altitude and situations causing hypoxemia; consider air travel planning if needed

required_evidence: Recurrent neutropenia in a periodic pattern (typically ~21-day cycle) with symptomatic episodes (e.g., infections, oral ulcers, fevers) during nadirs
Serial CBCs 2–3 times weekly for 6–8 weeks demonstrating cyclic ANC nadirs (often ANC < 0.2–0.5 x10^9/L) with recovery between episodes
Other cell lines relatively preserved compared with aplastic processes (platelets typically normal; hemoglobin usually near baseline unless intercurrent illness)
Clinical exclusion of alternative causes of neutropenia (drug-induced, autoimmune, marrow failure, acute leukemia/MDS) based on history, exam, and labs
Differential consideration: if syndromic features (developmental delay, periodic fevers, immunodeficiency, arthritis) are present, pursue targeted genetic/immune evaluation

expected_lab_results.WBC: Fluctuates with cycles; low during neutropenic nadirs and returns toward baseline between episodes
expected_lab_results.Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Marked periodic drops (often <0.5 x10^9/L, may be <0.2 x10^9/L) with recovery to near-normal between nadirs; approximate 21-day periodicity on serial testing
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal or mildly decreased; not typically profoundly low unless another process is present
expected_lab_results.Hematocrit: Usually normal or mildly decreased, paralleling hemoglobin
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Typically normal (may show mild reactive increases during inflammatory/infectious episodes)
expected_lab_results.Absolute lymphocyte count: Generally normal; may appear relatively increased during neutropenic nadirs due to low ANC
expected_lab_results.Absolute monocyte count: May increase around/precede neutrophil recovery (relative monocytosis can be seen during nadirs)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Usually normal for age/baseline; may rise if concurrent anemia from intercurrent illness/hemolysis is present (not a defining feature)

medications: Filgrastim (G-CSF) 5 mcg/kg subcutaneous daily during neutropenic periods; titrate to maintain ANC >0.5 x10^9/L and minimize infections
Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO every 4-6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 60-75 mg/kg/day or per local guidance)
non_pharmacological: Obtain serial CBC with differential 2-3 times weekly for 6-8 weeks to confirm cyclic pattern and guide G-CSF dosing

Fever/infection plan: any temperature ≥38.0°C (100.4°F) during suspected/known neutropenia requires urgent evaluation; consider empiric IV broad-spectrum antibiotics per febrile neutropenia protocols if ANC <0.5 x10^9/L or clinically ill

Infection prevention counseling: meticulous hand hygiene, prompt dental/oral care for ulcers/gingivitis, avoid sick contacts when possible, keep immunizations up to date (inactivated vaccines as scheduled; live vaccines per clinician assessment and immune status)
Hematology referral for confirmation, genetic testing (e.g., ELANE) when appropriate, and long-term monitoring
Review current use of oprelvekin: not indicated for cyclic neutropenia; reassess necessity and discontinue if no clear platelet-related indication
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks; sooner (same day/within 24 hours) for fever or signs of infection during neutropenic nadir

must_include: Definition: cyclic neutropenia is a condition where neutrophil counts drop on a predictable cycle, increasing infection risk during low points
How diagnosis is confirmed: repeated CBCs over several weeks to show the cyclic pattern
What to watch for: fevers, mouth sores, sore throat, skin infections, and when to seek urgent care
Primary therapy is G-CSF (filgrastim) to reduce depth/duration of neutropenia and prevent infections
Oprelvekin is a platelet growth factor and does not treat neutropenia; plan to reassess its need
Monitoring plan: CBC monitoring and dose adjustment based on ANC and symptoms
Potential side effects of G-CSF (e.g., bone pain, headache; rare splenic enlargement/rupture—seek care for left upper quadrant pain)
Safety plan for fever: urgent evaluation for any fever during neutropenia; may need prompt antibiotics
Clarify allergy history and ensure antibiotics chosen for infections account for penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and latex sensitivity
Discuss differential diagnoses and that additional testing may be needed if developmental delay, periodic fevers outside neutropenic periods, immunodeficiency, or arthritis symptoms are present
Infection prevention measures (hand hygiene, dental care, avoiding exposure to illness when counts are low)
Keep a symptom/temperature log and, if available, a calendar of ANC nadirs to anticipate high-risk days
When to call immediately: fever ≥38.0°C, rigors, shortness of breath, worsening mouth ulcers, or any rapidly progressive infection signs

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for cyclic neutropenia/cyclic hematopoiesis: infection pattern, periodicity, severity, prior CBC trends, meds (including G-CSF/oprelvekin), family history, and red flags suggesting alternative diagnoses.

Order/plan serial CBC with differential (e.g., 2–3x/week for 6–8 weeks) to document cyclic ANC nadirs; include baseline labs as appropriate (e.g., CMP if considering meds, inflammatory markers if indicated).
Perform required allergy check before ordering any tests/supplies that may involve latex exposure (critical safety check) and before any subsequent prescribing.
Review CBC trend results to identify a predictable cyclic pattern of neutropenia and correlate with symptoms/infections.
Make/confirm diagnosis of cyclic neutropenia based on repeated CBCs showing periodic ANC drops and clinical correlation; consider and document key differentials if pattern is atypical.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment approach, monitoring, and urgent return precautions; provide infection prevention and self-monitoring guidance.
Perform required allergy check before prescribing (critical); specifically clarify penicillin reaction (urticarial rash) and latex sensitivity implications.
Perform drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical), especially if antibiotics or other supportive meds are being considered.
Initiate/adjust G-CSF (filgrastim) plan as primary therapy; avoid misusing oprelvekin for neutropenia and document reassessment of its indication; provide clear monitoring and dose-adjustment plan.
Arrange follow-up for CBC monitoring, symptom review, ANC-guided dose titration, and reassessment of infection frequency and medication need.
Close the encounter after ensuring safety-net instructions and follow-up are in place.

nl_assertions: The agent explains cyclic neutropenia as predictable cycles of low neutrophil counts with increased infection risk during nadirs, using patient-appropriate language.
The agent states that diagnosis is confirmed by repeated CBCs over several weeks demonstrating a cyclic ANC pattern (not by a single CBC).

The agent identifies G-CSF (filgrastim) as primary therapy to reduce depth/duration of neutropenia and prevent infections, and mentions common side effects (bone pain, headache) and rare splenic enlargement/rupture with LUQ pain warning.

The agent explicitly notes that oprelvekin is a platelet growth factor and does not treat neutropenia, and plans to reassess its ongoing need rather than presenting it as appropriate therapy for cyclic neutropenia.
The agent includes a monitoring plan with CBC/ANC tracking and dose adjustment based on ANC and symptoms.
The agent completes required safety checks: allergy check before ordering, allergy and contraindication checks before prescribing; failure of any critical check is scored as a critical error.
The agent clarifies allergy history and, when discussing infection treatment, accounts for penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and latex sensitivity in antibiotic/supply choices and counseling.

The agent addresses concerns by discussing differentials and triggers for additional testing if atypical features exist (developmental delay, periodic fevers outside neutropenic periods, immunodeficiency, arthritis symptoms).

The agent provides lifestyle/infection prevention education (hand hygiene, dental care, avoiding sick contacts during low counts) and recommends a symptom/temperature log and, if available, an ANC calendar to anticipate high-risk days.
The agent avoids unsafe reassurance (e.g., advising to ‘wait it out’ with fever during neutropenia) and recommends urgent evaluation and possible prompt antibiotics for fever during neutropenia.

The agent does not prescribe without performing allergy and contraindication checks; if prescribing antibiotics, the agent avoids penicillins in a patient with urticarial rash history unless appropriately evaluated/desensitized and offers suitable alternatives.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious but clear-speaking project coordinator. Open with: “I just can’t sleep, and it’s starting to mess with my life.” Volunteer: long time to fall asleep, frequent awakenings, unrefreshed mornings, daytime fatigue and poor focus at work. If asked, share: ~3 months, most nights; racing worries at bedtime; wake 3–4 AM and can’t return to sleep for 1+ hour; irritability; afternoon coffee; phone use in bed; melatonin helped briefly; sleep in on weekends then can’t sleep Sunday. Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about meds, substances, or other symptoms: occasional decongestant for allergies, after-work naps, nighttime heartburn (worse after late meals), little exercise/sunlight, weight gain/tight collar, nighttime urination 1–2 times. Resist/withhold at first: 2 drinks most nights to “take the edge off,” occasional leftover anxiety pill from a friend; admit if asked directly and express worry about dependence. Avoid sleep-apnea clues unless asked; if asked about snoring/gasping, reluctantly say partner noticed gasping/choking. Misconceptions: suspect thyroid, ADHD, or dementia; cite sleep app “light sleep” as alarming. If clinician is empathetic, soften, thank them, and become more open. Emphasize cost/time concerns; push for a single prescription and reluctance for therapy or follow-ups.

medications: Butobarbital 100 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Lisinopril 10 mg PO daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: SSRI-associated insomnia (sertraline)
High caffeine intake (2-3 coffees plus afternoon energy drink)
Perimenopausal symptoms with nocturnal hot flashes
Restless legs symptoms likely related to iron deficiency
Irregular sleep schedule due to rotating work shifts
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk (overweight, reports loud snoring)

required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Sleep disturbance causes clinically significant distress or daytime impairment (fatigue, mood issues, concentration problems, reduced performance)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week
Present for at least 3 months (or specify short-term insomnia if <3 months)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep are present
Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder (e.g., OSA, circadian rhythm disorder, RLS), substance/medication effect, or uncontrolled medical/psychiatric condition

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Within reference range (no anemia contributing to fatigue/sleep complaints)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal (≥50 ng/mL; no iron deficiency contributing to restless legs symptoms)

non_pharmacological: CBT-I as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)
Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time, avoid naps, limit caffeine after noon, avoid alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening screen time, keep bedroom dark/cool/quiet, bed only for sleep/sex
If awake >20 minutes, leave bed and do a quiet activity until sleepy, then return to bed
Review and address contributing factors: stress, anxiety/depression symptoms, pain, nocturia, GERD; screen for OSA and RLS based on history
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess sleep latency/wake after sleep onset, daytime function, adverse effects, and adherence; sooner (within 1 week) if medication side effects or safety concerns

must_include: Define insomnia and confirm it is based on symptoms plus daytime impairment
Clarify duration/frequency criteria and adequate sleep opportunity
Explain evaluation for common contributors (thyroid disease, iron deficiency/RLS, substances, OSA symptoms, mood/anxiety)
CBT-I is first-line and often more durable than medications
Medication plan is adjunctive, lowest effective dose, reassess regularly
Avoid or discontinue butobarbital for insomnia due to dependence, overdose/respiratory depression risk, next-day impairment, and guideline-disfavored use
Discuss risks of sedatives (falls, driving impairment, cognitive effects), especially if combined with alcohol/other CNS depressants

Shared decision-making about medication vs non-medication options and patient goals
Consistent wake time and limiting time in bed to consolidate sleep

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia complaint details (sleep onset/maintenance/early awakening), duration/frequency, adequate sleep opportunity, and daytime impairment; screen for red flags and common contributors (OSA symptoms, RLS/iron deficiency symptoms, thyroid symptoms, mood/anxiety, substances/meds).
Order targeted labs only if indicated by history/exam (e.g., TSH for thyroid symptoms; ferritin/iron studies if RLS/PLMD symptoms; CBC if anemia suspected).
Review any ordered lab results to refine differential and avoid inappropriate sedative prescribing when a reversible medical contributor is likely.
Make a working diagnosis of insomnia disorder when criteria are met (symptoms + daytime impairment, adequate opportunity, duration/frequency), and document likely contributors/comorbidities.
Explain diagnosis and evaluation rationale; provide CBT-I first-line counseling and sleep hygiene/stimulus control/sleep restriction basics; set expectations and use of a sleep diary.
Critical safety check before prescribing any medication for insomnia.
Critical safety check before prescribing; assess interactions with alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, antihistamines, and other CNS depressants.

Critical safety check before prescribing; consider pregnancy, severe hepatic impairment, history of substance use disorder, untreated OSA, severe COPD/respiratory depression risk, fall risk, older age, and complex sleep behaviors risk.

If medication is chosen as adjunctive therapy, prescribe an evidence-based option at lowest effective dose for shortest duration and explicitly avoid butobarbital for insomnia due to safety and guideline-disfavored use.

Provide medication safety counseling (7–8 hours available, avoid alcohol/CNS depressants, next-day impairment/driving precautions, fall risk) and reinforce CBT-I plan and lifestyle guidance (caffeine/alcohol/nicotine timing, consistent wake time, limit time in bed).
Arrange reassessment (typically 2–4 weeks) to review sleep diary, response, adverse effects, and to taper/stop hypnotics if used; escalate evaluation if persistent or red flags emerge.
Close encounter after summarizing plan and confirming understanding and shared decision-making.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 29-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly but sound scared. Start with: sudden, severe sharp lower belly pain began 1 hour ago “out of nowhere,” and you’re worried something tore. Volunteer: lightheaded when standing, bluish whites of eyes, and that you have vascular Ehlers-Danlos; emphasize fear this is serious. If asked, add: pain is mostly lower belly, comes in waves but never fully goes away; short of breath, weak, heart racing; easy bruising; nosebleeds hard to stop. Do not mention unless specifically asked: unusual new headaches; brief sharp chest/upper back pains; gums bleed easily; small cuts bleed longer. Withhold at first (only if asked directly about bleeding, bowel habits, or sex/gyne): bright red blood on wiping a couple times this month; painful sex and spotting after—admit you were embarrassed and afraid. If asked about other symptoms, mention shaky/sweaty when not eating and better after juice, weeks of fatigue/dizziness, intermittent blurry vision. Express cost concerns about imaging/follow-ups and worry genetic diagnosis could affect insurance. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and become more forthcoming.

medications: Celiprolol 200 mg PO twice daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
comorbidities: Iron deficiency anemia due to heavy menstrual bleeding
confounders: Blue sclerae can suggest osteogenesis imperfecta, potentially diverting evaluation away from vascular EDS
Easy bruising and menorrhagia with normal PT/aPTT can mimic von Willebrand disease or platelet function disorders
Orthostatic tachycardia (POTS) can be mistaken for anxiety/panic disorder and complicate assessment of hemodynamic instability
Intermittent chest/abdominal pain episodes may be misattributed to musculoskeletal pain or IBS despite risk of arterial/organ rupture

acceptable_alternatives: Heritable connective tissue disorder consistent with vEDS (COL3A1-related)
required_evidence: Blue sclerae noted on exam
History/assessment for vEDS features: easy bruising, thin translucent skin, characteristic facial features, arterial/intestinal/uterine fragility, family history of early arterial rupture

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal (no specific abnormality expected in vEDS); may be low only if recent/occult bleeding
expected_lab_results.CBC - Hematocrit: Typically normal; may be low only if bleeding
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Typically normal; may be low if iron deficiency from chronic blood loss
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Usually normal; may be low if iron deficiency from bleeding
expected_lab_results.Serum iron: Usually normal; may be low if iron deficiency
expected_lab_results.TIBC: Usually normal; may be high if iron deficiency

medications: No routine medication required for isolated blue sclerae; consider celiprolol 200 mg PO daily (titrate as tolerated) if vEDS confirmed and available per local guidelines/specialist
non_pharmacological: Urgent referral to medical genetics for confirmation (COL3A1 testing) and counseling
Vascular surgery/cardiology referral for baseline vascular imaging plan (e.g., CTA/MRA head-to-pelvis per specialist) and surveillance strategy
Activity modification: avoid contact sports/heavy lifting/straining; individualized exercise advice
Bleeding/rupture precautions: prompt emergency evaluation for sudden severe chest/abdominal/back pain, neurologic deficits, or unexplained swelling/bruising
Avoid unnecessary invasive procedures; if procedures needed, ensure experienced teams and careful technique
Pregnancy counseling (high maternal risk in vEDS); high-risk obstetrics if applicable
Family screening: offer cascade testing to first-degree relatives once variant identified
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Genetics appointment within 2-4 weeks; earlier (same day/ED) if any acute pain or signs of vascular event

Discuss family implications and option for relatives to be tested if a variant is found
Discuss procedural and trauma-related risks and how to communicate this to other clinicians
Invite questions about prognosis, activity limits, and pregnancy risks (if relevant)
Avoid high-impact/contact sports and heavy lifting/straining; choose low-impact activities
Carry medical alert information indicating suspected/confirmed vEDS and allergies (penicillin, latex)
Importance of regular follow-up and adhering to the agreed surveillance plan

I feel stiff when I wake up and it takes a while to loosen up

general_concerns: Believes her joint pain and swelling are mainly caused by eating certain foods (e.g., sugar, spicy foods) and thinks strict dieting can replace prescribed medicines.
Thinks juvenile arthritis is the same as 'wear-and-tear' arthritis and worries that using her joints will permanently 'grind them down,' so she avoids activity even when advised to keep moving.
Assumes the LACC1 genetic diagnosis means nothing can help and that treatment is pointless because it is 'in the DNA.'
Worries that lithium carbonate is being used to treat her arthritis directly and is afraid it will 'damage the kidneys' even at prescribed doses, so she sometimes skips it without telling anyone.
Believes injections/biologic medicines are only for 'very severe' cases and that starting them means she will become dependent or 'immune' to them.
economic_concerns: Thinks specialist visits and lab monitoring are optional and can be skipped to save money if symptoms are not severe that week.
Assumes genetic or biologic treatments will be unaffordable and avoids discussing them, expecting she will be turned away if she cannot pay.
Believes physical therapy is a luxury service and that home rest is the only realistic option due to transportation and missed-work costs for her caregiver.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 14-year-old anxious 8th-grade girl. Speak vaguely and worry you’re “making a big deal.” Start with: knees and ankles keep swelling, feel warm and sore, worse in the mornings with stiffness that takes a while to loosen. Mention you sometimes limp and you’ve been more tired.

Only if asked, add: flares (good weeks then sudden worse), wrists/fingers ache when writing/opening things, low fevers during bad days, low appetite with slight weight loss, occasional sore/light-sensitive eyes with flares, and mouth sores that go away.

Do not volunteer: slow growth, waking at night from throbbing, avoiding sports due to “locking/giving out,” easy bruising/nosebleeds, frequent lingering colds.

Resist discussing private/hip/pelvic stiffness and any bedwetting—deny or minimize until directly asked in a nonjudgmental way.

Express misconceptions: think it’s allergies/food triggers, family wonders about “periodic fevers,” and you suspect anemia. Raise cost worries: ask if specialist visits/labs can be skipped when you feel okay; assume biologics/PT are unaffordable and prefer resting at home.

If the clinician shows empathy, soften, give more detail, and admit you’re scared about walking and missing school/childcare.

medications: Lithium carbonate 300 mg PO twice daily
Naproxen 250 mg PO twice daily with food as needed for joint pain
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000 IU PO daily
comorbidities: Juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1
confounders: Fanconi anemia with baseline pancytopenia complicating assessment of systemic inflammation and limiting DMARD options (e.g., methotrexate)
Intermittent febrile episodes with neutropenia increase concern for infection versus inflammatory flare
Renal tubular dysfunction (glycosuria with normal serum glucose, low bicarbonate, hypophosphatemia) complicates NSAID use and affects lithium safety monitoring
Low BMI and hypoalbuminemia may reflect chronic inflammation or poor intake and can alter drug handling and symptom perception

diagnosis.primary: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1
differentials: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome

correct_diagnosis.primary: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis–like autoinflammatory arthritis due to LACC1 (FAMIN) deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: Monogenic juvenile arthritis due to LACC1 (FAMIN) deficiency
Systemic autoinflammatory arthritis due to LACC1 mutation
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (if genetic confirmation unavailable)
required_evidence: Childhood-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis (persistent joint swelling/pain with morning stiffness) consistent with juvenile arthritis phenotype
Inflammatory markers elevated during active disease (ESR and/or CRP)

Exclusion of key mimics suggested by available labs: no persistent neutropenia/cyclic ANC nadirs suggesting cyclic hematopoiesis; no microcytic anemia with iron overload pattern suggesting congenital sideroblastic anemia syndrome
Known or suspected pathogenic LACC1 variant (if available from history/genetics)

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Mild normocytic or anemia of chronic inflammation may be present (low-normal to mildly low hemoglobin); not a marked microcytic anemia pattern
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Typically normal (normocytic); not persistently low as in sideroblastic anemia
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated with inflammation; not showing cyclic severe leukopenia
expected_lab_results.CBC - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): Normal or mildly elevated; not cyclic profound neutropenia
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal to mildly elevated as an acute-phase reactant; not markedly elevated with iron overload pattern

medications: Naproxen 10 mg/kg/day PO divided BID (max 1000 mg/day) if tolerated
Methotrexate 10-15 mg/m^2 PO or SC once weekly
Folic acid 1 mg PO daily (or 5 mg PO once weekly, not on methotrexate day)
Adalimumab 24 mg/m^2 SC every 2 weeks (max 40 mg per dose) if inadequate response to methotrexate or moderate-severe disease
non_pharmacological: Rheumatology referral/ongoing co-management for monogenic arthritis
Physical and occupational therapy for range of motion, strengthening, and function
Heat/cold, activity pacing, and school accommodations as needed
Vaccination review prior to immunosuppression; avoid live vaccines while on biologic therapy
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess disease activity and labs in 4-6 weeks after initiating/adjusting DMARD; sooner (1-2 weeks) if worsening symptoms or medication adverse effects

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis starting in childhood; LACC1-related disease is a genetic (monogenic) form that can resemble juvenile idiopathic arthritis
Goal is controlling inflammation to prevent joint damage, pain, and disability
Differentials considered and why current labs/symptoms make cyclic neutropenia or sideroblastic anemia syndromes less likely
Stepwise approach: anti-inflammatory symptom control plus disease-modifying therapy (methotrexate) and escalation to biologic if needed
Methotrexate is weekly (not daily) and requires folic acid supplementation
Monitoring plan: periodic CBC and inflammatory markers; watch for infection signs on immunosuppressive therapy
NSAID choice accounts for ibuprofen intolerance; discuss GI protection and taking with food
Review penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and ensure antibiotics/prescriptions avoid penicillins unless formally evaluated
Discuss lithium carbonate: need to avoid dehydration and monitor for toxicity; NSAIDs can increase lithium levels—coordinate with prescriber and consider lithium level monitoring if NSAID used
When to seek urgent care: fever, severe infection symptoms, unusual bruising/bleeding, severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, or signs of lithium toxicity (tremor, confusion, diarrhea)
Encourage regular low-impact activity and stretching to preserve joint function
Sleep, balanced nutrition, and hydration (especially important with lithium)
Medication adherence and keeping scheduled lab monitoring appointments

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit current symptoms (joint pain/swelling/stiffness), duration, functional impact, fever/rash/uveitis symptoms, infection history, bleeding/bruising, GI symptoms, medication history (including lithium), and prior NSAID reaction details.

Order baseline and monitoring labs relevant to inflammatory arthritis and immunosuppression safety: CBC with differential, CMP/LFTs, ESR/CRP; consider pregnancy test if applicable; consider hepatitis B/C and TB screening before biologic therapy.
Review labs to support inflammatory arthritis activity, establish baseline for methotrexate, and address differentials (e.g., neutropenia pattern, anemia patterns).
State juvenile-onset chronic inflammatory arthritis consistent with LACC1-related monogenic arthritis/JIA-like disease; document reasoning and exclusion of key differentials given labs/symptoms.
Perform required critical allergy check before any prescribing; confirm penicillin allergy phenotype (urticarial rash) and document avoidance plan.
Perform required critical interaction check before prescribing, focusing on lithium-NSAID interaction and methotrexate interactions (e.g., TMP-SMX).
If an NSAID is selected, explicitly re-check interaction details and mitigation (lithium level monitoring, hydration, alternative analgesics).

Initiate stepwise therapy: NSAID for symptom control (avoiding ibuprofen due to intolerance) with GI protection advice; start methotrexate weekly with folic acid; outline escalation to biologic if inadequate response.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (weekly MTX + folate, monitoring), address concerns (penicillin allergy; lithium precautions and toxicity signs; urgent care triggers), and lifestyle guidance (activity, sleep, nutrition, hydration, adherence).
Arrange follow-up to assess response and adverse effects, review labs (CBC/LFTs/ESR/CRP), coordinate with lithium prescriber if NSAID used, and plan escalation if needed.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: VIPoma (pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor causing WDHA syndrome)
acceptable_alternatives: Verner-Morrison syndrome (WDHA syndrome) due to VIP-secreting neuroendocrine tumor
required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting serum VIP (typically >200 pg/mL, often much higher) in the setting of secretory watery diarrhea
Electrolyte pattern consistent with secretory diarrhea: hypokalemia and non–anion gap metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate) with hyperchloremia
Diarrhea persists with fasting and is large-volume watery
Improvement of diarrhea with somatostatin analog therapy (supportive evidence)
Exclude common infectious/medication causes of diarrhea and other endocrine causes (supportive context)

expected_lab_results.Serum VIP: Elevated, typically >200 pg/mL (often markedly elevated in VIPoma)
expected_lab_results.Bicarbonate (CO2): Low (non–anion gap metabolic acidosis) from bicarbonate-rich stool losses
expected_lab_results.Chloride: High/relative hyperchloremia consistent with non–anion gap metabolic acidosis
expected_lab_results.Sodium: Often low-normal; may be low with volume depletion
expected_lab_results.Magnesium: Low or low-normal (GI losses), may contribute to refractory hypokalemia
expected_lab_results.Calcium: Normal to mildly elevated (can be associated; also consider MEN1 context if elevated)
expected_lab_results.Glucose (fasting): Elevated or impaired fasting glucose (VIP can promote hyperglycemia)

medications: 0.9% sodium chloride IV bolus 1-2 L then titrate to euvolemia and urine output
Potassium chloride IV 10-20 mEq/hour (with continuous ECG monitoring if >10 mEq/hour) or PO as tolerated; recheck K frequently
Magnesium sulfate IV 1-2 g over 1 hour, repeat as needed to correct hypomagnesemia and facilitate K repletion
Octreotide 100-200 mcg subcutaneous every 8 hours (or 50-100 mcg SC q8h and titrate) for control of secretory diarrhea
Pantoprazole 40 mg PO/IV daily (supportive acid suppression if high-output diarrhea/ulcer risk; optional based on presentation)
non_pharmacological: Strict intake/output monitoring; daily weights; assess for dehydration and hemodynamic instability
Frequent electrolyte monitoring (K, bicarbonate/CO2, Mg, Na, Cl) and ECG monitoring when repleting potassium
Hold/avoid medications that worsen diarrhea or potassium loss (e.g., stimulant laxatives, unnecessary diuretics)

After stabilization: tumor localization and staging (contrast-enhanced CT/MRI abdomen; somatostatin receptor imaging such as Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) and referral to endocrinology/oncology/surgical oncology for definitive management (surgical resection if localized; systemic options if metastatic)
Consider MEN1 evaluation if clinical suspicion (history/family history; check calcium/PTH if hypercalcemia present)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24-48 hours for symptom control and electrolyte stabilization; specialty follow-up within 1-2 weeks for imaging/staging and definitive tumor-directed therapy planning.

must_include: VIPoma is a rare hormone-secreting neuroendocrine tumor that causes large-volume watery diarrhea
Diarrhea leads to dehydration and electrolyte/acid-base problems (low potassium and low bicarbonate)
Blood test showing high VIP supports the diagnosis; imaging is needed to find and stage the tumor
Immediate priorities are IV fluids and correcting potassium/magnesium and acid-base abnormalities
Octreotide reduces hormone release and typically improves diarrhea
Definitive treatment depends on tumor location/spread (surgery if possible; other therapies if metastatic)
Need for close lab monitoring and possible heart rhythm monitoring during potassium replacement
Review penicillin allergy and clarify reaction (urticarial rash) before giving any antibiotics
Red-flag symptoms requiring urgent care (worsening weakness, palpitations, confusion, fainting, inability to keep fluids down)
Discuss expected course: symptom improvement can occur quickly with octreotide, but tumor workup is still required
Hydration plan and oral rehydration when able; avoid alcohol/caffeine if they worsen diarrhea
Track stool frequency/volume and symptoms; bring log to follow-up
Dietary adjustments during active diarrhea (small frequent meals; avoid high-osmolar foods) while emphasizing this does not replace medical treatment

Order initial stabilization and diagnostic labs: CMP (Na/K/Cl/HCO3/Cr), Mg, Phos, glucose, CBC, VBG/ABG if severe, EKG if hypokalemia suspected, stool studies as appropriate, and VIP level (fasting) to support VIPoma.
Interpret labs for dehydration/AKI, hypokalemia, metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate), and confirm elevated VIP; use results to determine urgency and monitoring needs.
Synthesize presentation and labs to diagnose suspected VIPoma (WDHA syndrome) and outline need for imaging to localize/stage (CT/MRI pancreas, somatostatin receptor imaging).
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment priorities, address concerns and red flags, and provide lifestyle guidance including hydration and stool log.
Before any prescribing (especially antibiotics), verify allergies and clarify penicillin reaction (urticarial rash) to ensure safe selection.
Before prescribing, screen for clinically relevant interactions (e.g., octreotide effects on glucose, QT-risk meds in hypokalemia, concurrent diuretics). Non-critical but expected.

Initiate therapy consistent with suspected VIPoma and stabilization plan: IV fluids/rehydration strategy, electrolyte repletion plan, and octreotide to reduce secretory diarrhea; ensure monitoring recommendations are included.
Arrange urgent follow-up/transfer as needed for severe electrolyte derangements; otherwise schedule endocrine/oncology/surgery evaluation and imaging; plan close lab monitoring.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Intellectual developmental disorder (intellectual disability), requiring etiologic evaluation and screening for reversible contributors
acceptable_alternatives: Neurodevelopmental disorder with intellectual impairment (unspecified) pending formal cognitive/adaptive testing

required_evidence: History of onset during developmental period (childhood) with persistent limitations
Deficits in intellectual functioning documented or strongly supported by standardized testing or clinical assessment (e.g., reasoning, problem-solving, learning)
Deficits in adaptive functioning across conceptual, social, and/or practical domains that limit independent functioning
Not better explained by delirium, acute intoxication, major neurocognitive disorder, untreated hypothyroidism, or vitamin B12 deficiency
Assessment of comorbidities and reversible contributors (thyroid dysfunction, B12 deficiency, vitamin D deficiency; metabolic risk factors)

expected_lab_results.TSH: Typically normal in primary intellectual developmental disorder; abnormal (elevated) would suggest hypothyroidism as a contributing/reversible factor
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Typically normal; low would support hypothyroidism
expected_lab_results.Vitamin B12: Often normal; low would suggest potentially reversible cognitive/neurologic contribution and warrants replacement
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: Commonly low/insufficient in many patients; deficiency would warrant supplementation for bone health and fall risk reduction
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Variable; screen for diabetes/prediabetes (especially if overweight, sedentary, or on antipsychotics)
expected_lab_results.Lipid panel - Total cholesterol: Variable; assess ASCVD risk and need for lifestyle/therapy
expected_lab_results.Lipid panel - LDL-C: Variable; elevated LDL-C common and would drive lifestyle counseling and possible statin depending on age/risk

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily (if 25-OH vitamin D insufficient/deficient; dose adjust to level and local protocol)
Cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12) 1000 mcg PO daily (if B12 low or borderline with symptoms; consider IM regimen if malabsorption suspected)
Levothyroxine dose per weight/age and titration PO daily (only if hypothyroidism confirmed by elevated TSH with low free T4, or per guidelines for subclinical disease)
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis and severity with standardized cognitive testing and adaptive functioning assessment; document support needs

Etiologic workup as indicated by history/physical (e.g., hearing/vision screening, genetic evaluation when appropriate, review perinatal history, consider neuroimaging only if focal deficits, seizures, regression, abnormal neurologic exam)

Assess and treat comorbid psychiatric/behavioral conditions (ADHD, anxiety, depression, autism spectrum disorder) with evidence-based approaches; prioritize behavioral interventions first when feasible
Develop individualized support plan: special education/vocational services, occupational/speech therapy as indicated, social work/case management, guardianship/supported decision-making assessment
Safety planning: assess ability for medication management, driving, finances, home safety; screen for abuse/neglect and caregiver stress
Lifestyle counseling tailored to capacity and caregiver involvement: balanced diet, physical activity, sleep hygiene; address obesity/metabolic risk if present
Coordinate care with caregivers and multidisciplinary team; ensure accessible communication and reasonable accommodations

Discuss capacity and shared decision-making; clarify who can consent and how supported decision-making/guardianship works if relevant

Order labs to assess reversible contributors and common contributors to cognitive/functional impairment: TSH/free T4, vitamin B12, vitamin D (± CBC/CMP as clinically indicated). This is a noncritical contraindication check point before ordering if relevant (e.g., pregnancy status for certain tests not applicable here).

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis in accessible language with teach-back; explain treatment (no medication treats ID itself; focus on comorbidities and supports/therapies/community resources); address concerns (allergies, capacity/consent, prognosis/services/caregiver burden, crisis guidance); educate lifestyle (nutrition/activity, sleep/screen time, dental/metabolic risks, adherence supports if meds started).
If any medication is being considered (e.g., levothyroxine, vitamin supplementation, or comorbidity treatment), check drug-drug interactions with current meds/supplements.

Before prescribing, confirm contraindications relevant to the chosen medication(s) (e.g., levothyroxine cautions, hypervitaminosis risk, renal disease considerations for certain supplements) and ensure appropriateness for the patient.
Prescribe only if indicated by results (e.g., thyroid replacement or vitamin repletion) or to treat identified comorbidities; include monitoring plan and safety-net instructions.
Arrange follow-up to review response, monitoring labs (e.g., repeat TSH after starting levothyroxine), reassess functioning/support needs, and coordinate referrals/resources.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, documenting plan, and providing return precautions/crisis guidance.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly characterizes episodes as most consistent with nocturnal frontal lobe seizures (sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy) while acknowledging mimics and uncertainty pending confirmation.
Agent asks about semiology (sudden arousal, complex motor activity, dystonia, vocalization), stereotypy, timing within the night, duration, clustering, and post-event confusion to support diagnosis.
Agent screens for common mimics (NREM parasomnias, REM behavior disorder, panic, psychogenic events) and for obstructive sleep apnea symptoms when appropriate.
Agent elicits family history and explains autosomal dominant inheritance with incomplete penetrance in plain language.
Agent proposes confirmation strategies: home video capture, sleep study with expanded EEG or video-EEG monitoring, and explains why these help distinguish mimics.

Agent recommends carbamazepine as first-line for many patients, emphasizes starting low and titrating, and warns not to stop abruptly.

Agent discusses lifestyle triggers and prevention: consistent sleep, avoid sleep deprivation, limit alcohol, avoid recreational drugs, and review OTC/herbals for interactions.

Agent recommends home safety measures during sleep (remove sharp objects, consider low bed/padding, partner education) and documents a plan for breakthrough events including when to seek urgent care (prolonged or clustered seizures, injury, breathing issues).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose likely sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy and start carbamazepine after required safety checks, with counseling and follow-up.
must_collect: Nocturnal event semiology (sudden arousal, hypermotor movements, vocalization)
Timing in sleep and triggers (sleep deprivation, alcohol)
Daytime events or other seizure types
Family history of similar nocturnal events/epilepsy
Current medications including OTC/herbals and contraception
Comorbidities relevant to contraindications (hepatic disease, hyponatremia history, cardiac conduction issues if asked)
Red flags requiring urgent evaluation (prolonged seizure, status, respiratory compromise)
